questions.question: Define rationality in the context of AI and explain why it is preferred over human-like intelligence as a design goal.
keyPoints: Rationality is the property of making correct decisions based on available knowledge and information
It provides a measurable and achievable goal unlike human-like AI which is vague
Rationality can be systematically designed and evaluated

questions.question: Explain the difference between rationality and omniscience with an example.
keyPoints: Rationality: making best decision given available information
Omniscience: knowing all possible outcomes in advance
Example: A chess program that evaluates positions based on game state (rational) vs one that knows the exact outcome of every move (omniscient)

questions.question: What is the PEAS framework? Explain each component with an example of an automated taxi.
keyPoints: PEAS = Performance measure, Environment, Actuators, Sensors
Performance: Safe, fast, legal, comfortable ride
Environment: Roads, traffic, pedestrians, weather
Actuators: Steering, brakes, accelerator, signals
Sensors: Cameras, GPS, speedometer, lidar

questions.question: Differentiate between a simple reflex agent and a goal-based agent with a suitable example.
keyPoints: Simple reflex: condition-action rules, no consideration of future
Goal-based: considers sequences of actions to achieve goals
Example: Thermostat as reflex, GPS navigation as goal-based

questions.question: What is autonomy in agents? How does it relate to rationality?
keyPoints: Autonomy: ability to learn from experience and rely on own knowledge
Autonomous agents are not entirely dependent on pre-programmed knowledge
Rational autonomous agents can improve performance over time through learning

questions.question: Explain the concept of information gathering in rational agents. Why is it important?
keyPoints: Information gathering: using sensors to perceive environment and build knowledge
Agents need current environment information to make rational decisions
Without proper information, agent cannot make appropriate decisions even with good reasoning

questions.question: Describe the different types of agents and their increasing capabilities.
keyPoints: Simple reflex: rule-based responses to current state
Goal-based: plans sequences to achieve goals
Utility-based: evaluates desirability of states using utility function
Learning: improves performance through experience

questions.question: Define a rational agent. Explain how rationality depends on the performance measure, percept sequence, and available knowledge with examples.
keyPoints: Rational agent: does the right thing based on available information
Performance measure defines success criteria
Rationality depends on sequence of percepts seen so far
Given same information, rational agent should always make same optimal choice
Example: Vacuum cleaner agent with different percept histories leads to different but rational actions
Important: rationality is about decision process, not outcomes

questions.question: Design a rational agent for playing tic-tac-toe. Specify the PEAS components, agent type, and explain how it makes rational decisions.
keyPoints: Performance: Win game, avoid losing, draw if necessary
Environment: 3x3 grid, opponent moves
Actuators: Place X in empty cell
Sensors: See current board state
Agent type: Goal-based (wins) or Utility-based (maximizes win probability)
Rational decisions based on: current board state, possible moves, opponent's likely responses
Uses game tree search or learned strategy

questions.question: Explain the four main types of agents in increasing order of sophistication. Discuss their advantages, limitations, and suitable applications.
keyPoints: Simple reflex: fast but limited, good for fully observable environments, suitable for simple tasks
Goal-based: handles complex planning but computationally expensive, suitable when future matters
Utility-based: handles trade-offs between goals, provides flexibility, suitable for complex real-world decisions
Learning: improves over time, adapts to new situations, suitable for dynamic environments
Each type represents progression toward more rational and capable agents

questions.question: Why is the concept of rationality fundamental to AI? Discuss how it provides a practical approach to building intelligent systems.
keyPoints: Rationality provides measurable, achievable goals rather than vague human-like intelligence
Allows systematic design and evaluation of agents
Defines clear success criteria through performance measures
Can be formally reasoned about and implemented
Provides foundation for all agent architectures
Key insight: rational action depends on knowledge, not outcome - good decision can lead to bad result due to unknown factors
